questions.question_text: Increase $\$120$ by $15\%$, then increase the result by another $15\%$.

options.why: Added the changes to a single $30\%$ increase, $\$120 \times 1.3$, instead of multiplying the multipliers.
Applied only one $15\%$ increase instead of both.
Increased by $\$30$ overall, treating repeated change as additive.

questions.question_text: A television costs $\$500$. Its price increases by $10\%$, then increases by another $10\%$. Find the new price.

options.why: Added the two increases to $20\%$ and applied $\$500 \times 1.2$.
Applied only one $10\%$ increase instead of both.
Added $\$110$, treating each $10\%$ as $10\%$ of a fixed $\$500$.

questions.question_text: A price increases by $10\%$, then increases by another $10\%$. Find the overall percentage change.

options.why: Added the two percentages instead of multiplying the multipliers.
Read the combined multiplier as the change without subtracting $100\%$.
Multiplied the multipliers but subtracted from the wrong base.

questions.solution_text:
Each $10\%$ increase is $\times 1.1$.
$1.1 \times 1.1 = 1.21$
$1.21 = 121\%$, so the overall change is $121\% - 100\% = 21\%$ increase.

questions.question_text: In a sale, prices are reduced by $20\%$, then a further $25\%$ is taken off the sale price. Find the overall percentage reduction.

options.why: Added the two discounts instead of multiplying the multipliers.
Subtracted the discounts from each other rather than combining multipliers.
Read the combined multiplier $0.6$ directly as the reduction instead of $100\% - 60\%$.

questions.solution_text:
The $20\%$ reduction is $\times 0.8$; the $25\%$ reduction is $\times 0.75$.
$0.8 \times 0.75 = 0.6$
$0.6 = 60\%$, so the overall reduction is $100\% - 60\% = 40\%$.

questions.question_text: A number is increased by $50\%$, then the result is decreased by $50\%$. Compared with the original number, the final value is:

options.text: equal to the original, since the $50\%$ increase and $50\%$ decrease cancel
options.why: The decrease acts on the larger amount, so the two changes do not cancel.

options.text: greater than the original, since an increase came first
options.why: Order does not make the product exceed $1$; the combined multiplier is $0.75$.
Treats repeated change as additive rather than multiplying the multipliers.

questions.solution_text:
The $50\%$ increase is $\times 1.5$; the $50\%$ decrease is $\times 0.5$.
$1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75$
The final value is $75\%$ of the original — less than the original.
